Selling mutual fund and buying equivalent ETF: Can I 1031 exchange? Selling mutual fund and buying equivalent ETF: Can I 1031 exchange? Selling mutual fund and buying equivalent ETF: Can I 1031 exchange? No, you cannot use a 1031 exchange when selling a mutual fund and buying an equivalent ETF. Under IRS rules, a 1031 exchange, also known as a like-kind exchange, applies only to investment or business real estate. You cannot use a 1031 exchange for securities such as stocks, bonds, mutual funds, or ETFs. If you sell a mutual fund and want to buy a similar ETF, you will typically trigger capital gains taxes on the sale of the mutual fund, and cannot defer those taxes by purchasing the ETF through a 1031 exchange.

I have more than $250,000 in a US Bank account… mistake? I have more than $250,000 in a US Bank account… mistake? Having more than $250,000 in a US bank account can be risky from an insurance standpoint. The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) insures deposits at most US banks up to $250,000 per depositor, per account ownership category, per bank. This means that if the bank fails, amounts above $250,000 for a single depositor in the same ownership category are **not insured** and could be lost.  If you have more than $250,000 in one bank account in a single ownership category (such as an individual account), the portion above $250,000 is not covered by FDIC insurance.

Some common reasons include increased trading volume due to speculation, coordinated buying or selling by groups of investors on social media or online forums, or manipulation by traders attempting to “pump and dump” the stock for quick profits. Why do stocks split? Why do stocks split? Why do stocks split? Why do stocks split? Why do stocks split? A stock split occurs when a company increases the number of its shares outstanding by issuing more shares to current shareholders, usually in a specific ratio such as 2-for-1 or 3-for-1. The main reason companies split their stocks is to make the stock more affordable and attractive to a wider range of investors by lowering the price per share, without changing the company’s overall value. Stock splits can also increase the stock’s liquidity, making it easier to buy and sell shares. The total market value of the investment remains the same after a split, but each shareholder owns more shares at a proportionally lower price per share.

Why do put option prices go higher when the underlying stock tanks (drops)? Put option prices go higher when the underlying stock drops because puts give the holder the right to sell the stock at a predetermined price, known as the strike price. When the stock's market price falls below the strike price, the intrinsic value of the put option increases, since exercising the option allows the holder to sell the stock for more than its current market value. Additionally, as the stock price declines, investors often seek protection against further losses by buying puts, increasing demand and, in turn, pushing up put prices. This combination of higher intrinsic value and increased demand causes put option prices to rise when the underlying stock "tanks.

Does financing a portfolio on margin affect the variance of a portfolio? Yes, financing a portfolio on margin does affect the variance of a portfolio. When you use margin, you are borrowing money to increase your investment, which amplifies both potential gains and potential losses. This leverage increases the overall risk of the portfolio. As a result, the variance (a measure of the portfolio's risk or volatility) also increases. In general, the higher the proportion of the portfolio that is financed with borrowed funds, the greater the variance will be compared to an unleveraged portfolio. This is because both positive and negative returns are magnified by the use of margin.
